era_context: Mid-14th century: The Black Death pandemic continues to ravage Eurasia and North Africa, fundamentally disrupting demographic, economic, and social structures across Europe, the Islamic world, and Asia. The Hundred Years' War between England and France is in its early phase. The Ming Dynasty is consolidating power in China after the collapse of Mongol Yuan rule. The Ottoman Empire is expanding in Anatolia and the Balkans. Islamic sultanates dominate the Mediterranean and Indian Ocean trade.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically documented)
Indigenous Americas (no contemporary written records in European tradition)
Central Asian steppes (sparse documentation outside Chinese and Islamic sources)

events.title: Battle of Poitiers aftermath and English consolidation in Aquitaine
events.description: Following the English victory at Poitiers in 1356, English forces under Edward III and the Black Prince consolidate territorial gains in southwestern France. The Treaty of Brétigny (1360) will formalize these conquests, but 1351 marks a period of English military dominance and French internal disorder. The Hundred Years' War enters a phase of English ascendancy, with significant implications for feudal politics and dynastic legitimacy in Western Europe.
key_figures: Edward, the Black Prince (English military commander)
events.certainty_note: The broader military campaign is well-documented; specific events of 1351 are embedded in multi-year narratives with some dating ambiguity.

events.title: Black Death mortality peak in Western Europe
events.region: Western Europe (England, France, Low Countries, Iberia)
events.description: The Black Death, which arrived in Europe in 1347–1348, reaches peak mortality in many regions by 1351. Estimates suggest 30–60% population loss in affected areas. Labor shortages drive wage inflation and social unrest. Flagellant movements emerge as religious responses to the plague. Scapegoating of Jewish communities intensifies, leading to pogroms across the Rhineland and Central Europe. The demographic catastrophe reshapes feudal labor relations and accelerates the decline of serfdom.
key_figures: Pope Clement VI (attempted protection of Jews)
Various flagellant leaders (unnamed in most sources)

sources.name: Giovanni Boccaccio, The Decameron (written 1353, describes 1348 Florence)

events.certainty_note: Plague presence and mortality are well-attested in contemporary chronicles and later compilations. Exact mortality percentages are debated, but the scale of death is undisputed.

events.title: Zhu Yuanzhang's early military campaigns in southern China
events.region: Anhui and Jiangsu provinces, southern China
events.description: Zhu Yuanzhang, a former Buddhist monk and rebel leader, continues to consolidate power in the lower Yangtze region. By 1351, he has established himself as a major warlord opposing Yuan Mongol rule. His forces capture key cities and recruit talented administrators, including Xu Da and Liu Bowen. These campaigns lay the foundation for the Ming Dynasty, which will formally replace Yuan in 1368. Zhu's meritocratic recruitment of talent—regardless of social origin—distinguishes his movement from other rebel factions.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of Zhu's rise is well-documented in Ming official histories, but specific campaigns in 1351 are sometimes conflated with adjacent years in sources.

events.title: Ottoman expansion under Murad I in the Balkans
events.description: Murad I, the third Ottoman sultan, continues the Ottoman expansion into the Balkans, capturing key fortresses and establishing Ottoman administrative control. The Ottoman state, which emerged in Anatolia in the early 14th century, is now a major regional power. Murad's campaigns against Serbian and Bulgarian kingdoms establish Ottoman dominance that will persist for centuries. The conquest of Adrianople (Edirne) in 1361 will serve as a new Ottoman capital, but the groundwork is laid in campaigns like those of 1351.
key_figures: Serbian and Bulgarian regional rulers (various, often unnamed in Ottoman sources)
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in this period is well-attested, but Ottoman sources are often written centuries later. Specific dating of individual campaigns is sometimes uncertain.

events.title: Peasant unrest and labor shortage responses in England
events.description: Post-plague labor shortages drive English peasants to demand higher wages and better conditions. The English crown and nobility respond with the Statute of Labourers (1351), which attempts to freeze wages at pre-plague levels and restrict peasant mobility. This legislation, though largely ineffective, marks an early attempt to use state power to counter market forces created by demographic collapse. The statute will become a focal point of grievance leading to the Peasants' Revolt of 1381.

sources.name: Statute of Labourers (1351), English parliamentary record

events.certainty_note: The statute is a primary legal document with precise dating. Its passage and intent are unambiguous.

events.title: Jewish pogroms in the Rhineland and Central Europe
events.description: Scapegoating of Jewish communities for the Black Death intensifies in 1351. Pogroms occur across the Rhineland, with entire communities massacred or expelled. Despite papal protection efforts by Clement VI, local authorities and mobs target Jewish quarters. Thousands are killed; many communities are destroyed. This represents one of the worst periods of medieval anti-Jewish violence and will have lasting demographic and cultural consequences for European Jewry. Survivors migrate eastward to Poland and the Ottoman Empire.
key_figures: Local German princes and bishops (varied responses)
Jewish community leaders (largely unnamed in surviving sources)
events.certainty_note: Pogroms are well-documented in multiple contemporary sources, though specific casualty figures vary.

disconfirming_evidence: The Statute of Labourers (1351) is often portrayed as a successful policy response to labor shortages, but modern scholarship emphasizes its ineffectiveness—wages continued to rise despite the statute, and enforcement was sporadic. The statute's real significance lies in its symbolic assertion of state power and its role in generating grievance, not in achieving its stated goal. Additionally, while the Black Death is often dated to 1347–1348 arrival, some scholars argue that plague mortality in 1351 was declining in some regions (particularly Italy) even as it peaked in others (England, France), suggesting regional variation that oversimplified narratives often obscure.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1351 is moderate to good for Western Europe and the Islamic Mediterranean, but thinner for Asia and absent for most of Africa and the Americas. Western sources (particularly English and French chronicles) dominate, creating a potential bias toward European events. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu, Yuan histories) provide information on East Asia but are official compilations written after the fact. Ottoman sources are even later. The Black Death is perhaps over-represented in Western historiography relative to other significant events of the year. Jewish perspectives on the pogroms survive primarily through later memorial traditions rather than contemporary written records.

graph_edges.note: Labor shortages from plague mortality drive the Statute of Labourers
Plague mortality and social panic fuel scapegoating of Jewish communities
Zhu Yuanzhang's military consolidation in 1351 is foundational to Ming Dynasty establishment
Statute of Labourers becomes a focal grievance for the Peasants' Revolt
